planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Little Gem is one of the few magnolia cultivars that can be maintained as a large shrub or espalier-trained against a wall for a completely different landscape effect.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Little Gem is the single most important and most widely planted named cultivar of the Southern magnolia — a compact, narrow-form selection introduced by Warren Steed of Steed's Nursery in Candor, North Carolina, that has transformed the usability of this iconic Southern tree for residential landscapes. The standard Southern magnolia (Magnolia grandiflora) can reach 60–80 feet and 30–40 feet wide, making it a poor choice for typical modern residential lots. Little Gem reaches 15–25 feet tall with a dense, narrow spread of 7–10 feet — fitting foundation plantings, narrow screening situations, and typical residential lots that the species magnolia would overwhelm. Most remarkably, Little Gem begins blooming at 2–3 years of age — compared to 10–15 years for seed-grown species trees — producing medium-sized (3–4 inch), intensely fragrant white flowers from May through fall in zone 9 with periodic bloom throughout the growing season. The leaves have the characteristic deep green upper surface and rich cinnamon-brown woolly undersurface (indumentum) of the species. Little Gem also makes an outstanding espalier subject, pruned flat against a fence or wall to create a formal evergreen screen. Plant in well-drained, fertile soil; Southern magnolias dislike wet feet.

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; Kay Parris performs well in partial shade but blooms most prolifically with 6+ hours of direct sun.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Kay Parris is a named Southern magnolia cultivar selected for its superior cold hardiness, narrow columnar form, and early and prolific bloom — a combination of attributes that makes it one of the finest magnolias for zone 6–10 residential landscapes. The naturally narrow, upright habit (20–30 feet tall with a relatively tight 10–15 foot spread) fits medium-sized residential landscapes better than the broadly spreading species without requiring the extremely compact size of Little Gem. Kay Parris begins blooming at young age, producing large (6–8 inch), intensely fragrant white flowers from May through September in zone 9. The cultivar was selected and named by nurseryman David Parris for his wife Kay. A notable characteristic is the exceptionally cold-hardy performance for a Southern magnolia — Kay Parris has been successfully grown into zone 6 with protection, significantly colder than most Southern magnolia selections. The leaves show the characteristic glossy dark-green upper surface and reddish-bronze to cinnamon undersurface. The narrow form suits planting near buildings, driveways, and in limited spaces where the standard Southern magnolia would eventually overwhelm the landscape.

common_name: Bracken's Brown Beauty Magnolia (Cold-Hardy Southern Magnolia)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; Bracken's Brown Beauty is the hardiest widely available Southern magnolia cultivar and is the best choice where zone 5–6 cold hardiness is needed.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; Bracken's Brown Beauty is one of the most exceptional named Southern magnolia cultivars for cold-climate performance — consistently hardy in zone 5 with reliable protection and zone 6 without protection, significantly colder than most Southern magnolia cultivars. It was selected by Ray Bracken of Bracken's Greenhouses in Easley, South Carolina, from seedlings grown at the Secrest Arboretum in Wooster, Ohio — selected specifically for cold hardiness in a climate where Southern magnolias are not traditionally grown. Despite its northern origin, it performs beautifully in zone 9 and throughout the Southeast. The leaves are particularly distinctive: smaller and more densely arranged than many cultivars, with an exceptionally rich, dark cinnamon-brown to rusty-red woolly undersurface (indumentum) that is thicker and more prominent than most selections, creating year-round visual interest as the wind moves the leaves to reveal the contrasting undersides. The medium-sized flowers (5–7 inches) are intensely fragrant and produced prolifically from May through September in zone 9. Bracken's Brown Beauty grows to full tree size (20–30+ feet) and is appropriate for medium-to-large landscape plantings.

common_name: Southern Catalpa (Cigar Tree, Indian Bean Tree, Hardy Catalpa)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, fertile soil in full sun; Southern catalpa is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions including clay, sandy, and temporarily flooded soils.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, fertile soil in full sun; Southern catalpa is a fast-growing, bold-textured native deciduous tree of the southeastern United States — native to the Gulf Coastal Plain and Mississippi Delta region — producing enormous, heart-shaped, bright-green leaves up to 12 inches wide that create a dramatic tropical effect when backlit. In late spring and early summer, the tree produces spectacular upright clusters (panicles) of white, orchid-like flowers marked with yellow and purple spots, followed by distinctive long, narrow, cigar-like seed pods that reach 8–18 inches long and persist through winter, giving the tree its common names of 'cigar tree' and 'Indian bean tree.' The bold, tropical-looking foliage creates exceptional shade and the flower clusters are among the most beautiful of any North American native tree. The fallen flowers and seed pods can create some litter, so placement should account for cleanup. Southern catalpa is historically famous as catfish bait tree — the large catalpa sphinx moth caterpillars that feed on the leaves are prized as fishing bait. The tree adapts readily to the wide range of soil conditions found across the Gulf Coastal Plain, including heavy clay and periodically wet sites.
sources: USDA Forest Service Trees of the Southeast

common_name: Encore Autumn Bravo Azalea (Reblooming White Azalea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Autumn Bravo is one of the few pure white reblooming azaleas, making it valuable for formal gardens, white-themed borders, and mixed plantings where strong colors are used and a white accent is needed.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), humus-rich soil in partial shade with 4–6 hours of direct sun; Encore Autumn Bravo is the standard white-flowered Encore azalea cultivar — producing pure white, semi-double flowers with a light touch of pink at the petal edges in spring and again in substantial summer and fall bloom flushes. White-flowered reblooming azaleas are less common than pink, red, and purple reblooming selections, making Autumn Bravo particularly valuable for garden designs that require a white azalea with the extended bloom season of the Encore series. The pure white flowers provide maximum contrast with deep green foliage and make outstanding companions for dark-purple or deep-red flowering companions like Autumn Royalty or Autumn Fire. Autumn Bravo grows as a compact, well-rounded shrub to 2.5–3.5 feet tall and wide, suitable for mixed shrub borders, foundation plantings, and large containers. It shows the Encore series' characteristic requirement for more sun than traditional azaleas — at least 4 hours of direct sun is necessary to achieve reliable summer and fall rebloom. In deep shade, spring bloom may be adequate but fall rebloom will be sparse. The semi-evergreen foliage holds well through zone 9 winters.
